points.text: Profile the endpoint to measure each call's latency and identify which calls can be parallelized or batched.
Combine independent sequential API/database calls into async/parallel tasks to reduce overall wall‑clock time.
Analyze the SQL execution plan; add indexes on columns used in JOIN predicates and WHERE filters.
Rewrite the query to select only needed columns, replace SELECT * and eliminate unnecessary joins.
Apply early filtering (WHERE clauses) before joins or use CTEs to limit row sets early in the query.
Consider materialized views or caching for static reference data that is joined frequently.
Set a realistic endpoint timeout (e.g., 5‑10 s) based on measured 95th‑percentile DB response plus buffer.
Implement retry with exponential backoff for transient DB timeouts, respecting the overall timeout budget.
Tune the DB connection pool size to match expected concurrency without exhausting resources.
Parallelize independent service calls using async/await or thread pools to reduce overall latency.
Batch multiple calls into a single request when possible to cut round‑trip overhead.
Rewrite the SQL join to fetch only needed columns and add indexes on join keys.
Consider denormalizing or using materialized views for frequently accessed reporting data.
Set the endpoint timeout to the sum of 95th‑percentile latencies of each downstream call plus a safety margin (e.g., 20%).
Add a circuit breaker around each downstream call to fail fast and protect the reporting service.
Move sequential API calls into parallel async tasks to reduce overall latency.
Profile the query with EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) to identify slow joins and missing indexes.
Add covering indexes on foreign key columns used in join predicates and filter conditions.
Replace many-to-many joins with pre‑aggregated materialized view refreshed off‑peak.
Rewrite complex joins using explicit JOIN order or CTEs to let planner choose better plan.
Limit result set with pagination or aggregate in DB instead of pulling full dataset.
Set statement_timeout (e.g., 30s) and client‑side timeout budget (e.g., 60s) with fallback handling.
Batch the sequential calls into a single request or use a stored procedure to reduce round‑trips.
Add covering indexes on join/filter columns to avoid full table scans.
Rewrite the join query: push predicates early, replace unnecessary outer joins with inner joins, and consider CTEs for readability.
Profile the query plan; eliminate redundant tables or compute aggregates in a materialized view.
Set the endpoint timeout to the 95th‑percentile query latency plus a safety margin (e.g., 2× typical execution time).
Parallelize independent service calls to shrink the end‑to‑end critical path latency.
Rewrite the SQL to select only needed columns and replace heavy joins with pre‑aggregated materialized views.
Create indexes on join and filter columns; avoid SELECT * to reduce I/O and improve join performance.
Introduce caching or summary tables for frequently requested report data, refreshed on a schedule.
Measure p95 latency of DB and each service; allocate timeout budget (e.g., 70% DB, 20% services, 10% network).
Set the endpoint timeout slightly above the summed component budgets (p95 + ~10% margin) to prevent premature timeouts.
